questions.prompt: A network has two hidden layers with 256 neurons each. All weights in both layers are initialized to zero. What happens during training?

options.text: Training proceeds normally — zero weights are a neutral starting point that the optimizer can move away from
Every neuron in each layer learns the same function, so the layers behave as if they each had a single neuron
Gradients explode because all 256 neurons add identical contributions
The network converges more slowly but reaches the same solution as random initialization

questions.explanation: Zero initialization causes the symmetry problem. Every neuron in a layer has identical weights, produces identical outputs, and receives identical gradients — they all update identically. At every step, they remain identical. 256 neurons with zero init behave exactly like 1 neuron. The only way to break symmetry is to start from different points, which requires random initialization.
questions.hint: If every neuron starts identical, they receive identical gradients. What happens to the diversity of neurons after an update?

questions.prompt: Weights are initialized from $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ in a 100-layer network where each layer has 512 inputs. Approximately what is the variance of activations at layer 100, assuming no activation function?

options.text: $512^{100}$ — activations explode to an astronomically large number
$1$ — variance is preserved because the weights have unit variance
$1/512$ — variance shrinks because 512 weights average out
$0$ — activations collapse because many weights cancel

questions.explanation: For a linear layer with $n$ inputs and $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ weights: $\text{Var}(z) = n \cdot \text{Var}(w) \cdot \text{Var}(h) = 512 \cdot 1 \cdot \text{Var}(h)$. Each layer multiplies the variance by 512. After 100 layers: $512^{100} \approx 10^{271}$. This is why naïve $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ initialization fails — the stability condition $\sigma^2 = 1/n = 1/512$ is violated.
questions.hint: Each layer multiplies the variance by $n \cdot \sigma^2 = 512 \cdot 1 = 512$. What does multiplying by 512 one hundred times give?

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a legitimate use of `nn.init.zeros_()` or `tf.keras.initializers.Zeros()`?

options.text: Initializing the weight matrix of an nn.Linear layer
Initializing the bias vector of an nn.Linear layer
Initializing the weight matrix of an nn.Conv2d layer
Initializing the embedding table of an nn.Embedding layer

questions.explanation: Zero initialization is safe for **biases** because the bias is a single additive scalar per neuron — there is no symmetry to break between neurons via the bias term. The weight matrix is responsible for differentiating between neurons; the bias just shifts the activation threshold. Zero bias init means the initial decision boundary passes through the origin, which is a reasonable default.
questions.hint: The symmetry problem applies when multiple neurons share the same role. Biases are per-neuron, not shared.

questions.prompt: Xavier initialization uses the variance formula $\text{Var}(w) = 2 / (\text{fan\_in} + \text{fan\_out})$. For a `Linear(128, 256)` layer, what is the standard deviation for Xavier normal initialization?
questions.explanation: For `Linear(128, 256)`: fan_in = 128, fan_out = 256, fan_in + fan_out = 384. Xavier normal standard deviation = $\sqrt{2 / 384} \approx 0.072$. The uniform variant uses the bound $\sqrt{6 / 384} \approx 0.125$, which gives the same variance (since $\text{Var}(\mathcal{U}[-a, a]) = a^2/3$).

questions.prompt: Truncated normal initialization is preferred over standard normal initialization in Transformer models (e.g. ViT) primarily because:

options.text: It has a higher variance, which helps attention weights spread more broadly at initialization
It eliminates extreme outlier values (beyond ±2σ) that can cause initialization-time activation spikes
It is faster to compute since it avoids the exponential function in the Gaussian PDF
It sets all values greater than σ to exactly σ, making the initialization more deterministic

questions.explanation: Truncated normal resamples any value that falls outside $[\text{mean} \pm 2\sigma]$ (by default), eliminating the tails of the Gaussian. In very wide Transformer layers, a small fraction of large-magnitude weights can cause the first forward pass to produce extreme attention logits, destabilizing softmax. Truncating at ±2σ prevents this without changing the mean or most of the distribution. The TF Keras HeNormal and LecunNormal initializers use truncated normal internally for this reason.
questions.hint: Think about what happens in a wide layer (e.g. 768 inputs) when even one or two weights are very large.

questions.prompt: Complete the code to apply Xavier uniform initialization to all `nn.Linear` weight matrices and zero-initialize all biases in `model`:
questions.explanation: `nn.init.xavier_uniform_` initializes the tensor in-place using the uniform variant of Xavier/Glorot initialization. The trailing underscore is PyTorch's convention for in-place operations. `model.apply(fn)` recursively visits every submodule and applies `fn` — a clean way to initialize all layers of a model with consistent settings.
questions.hint: PyTorch in-place init functions end with an underscore. Xavier's uniform variant is `xavier_uniform_`.
